Trends:
- Rapid Africanization and expansion of the bureaucracy, police, army, schools and local administration after self-government/independence — strengthened the state.
- Copperbelt urbanization, mine employment, trade unions and UNIP/ANC party organization created a politically mobilized African working class — strengthened society, though later increasingly incorporated into the party-state.
- Heavy fiscal dependence on copper revenues and foreign-owned mining houses — strengthened the state when prices/revenues were high, but left it vulnerable to external shocks and mining capital.
- Growing UNIP dominance, patronage, use of detention/emergency powers, and weakening of multiparty competition before the formal one-party state — strengthened the state/party, weakened autonomous society.
- Centralization against chiefs and regional autonomies, especially Barotseland/Lozi institutions — strengthened the central state, weakened local society.
- Regional insecurity after Rhodesian UDI and white-minority regimes to the south/east forced militarization and transport rerouting — strengthened coercive state capacity, strained society and the economy.

Events:
- 1961 UNIP “Cha-cha-cha” civil-disobedience and sabotage campaign against colonial constitutional arrangements — strengthened nationalist society; pressured the colonial state.
- 1962 Northern Rhodesian elections and UNIP–ANC African majority coalition — strengthened organized African society and shifted power away from settler/federal interests.
- 1963 dissolution of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland — strengthened the emerging Northern Rhodesian/Zambian state; weakened settler-dominated federal constraints.
- January and October 1964 elections, internal self-government, and independence as Zambia under Kenneth Kaunda — greatly strengthened the sovereign state; nationalist society entered government.
- 1964 Barotseland Agreement, followed by later erosion of its guarantees — initially strengthened Lozi regional institutions; subsequent centralization weakened them.
- 1964 Lumpa Church uprising and state suppression/ban of the church — strengthened coercive state power; weakened an autonomous religious movement.
- 1965 Rhodesian UDI and Zambia’s emergency regulations — strengthened executive/security powers; weakened civil liberties and independent political space.
- 1968 Mulungushi Economic Reforms: state acquisition of majority stakes in major non-mining firms — strengthened state economic power; weakened private/foreign business autonomy.
- 1968 general election amid UNIP–ANC rivalry and violence — showed remaining societal/electoral competition, but also encouraged tighter party-state control.
- 1969 Matero Reforms and 1970 creation/expansion of state holding structures for mining/parastatals — strengthened state control over the commanding heights; weakened mining companies as counterweights.
